3  a
v Compensation of long input delays for linear systems can be  a
4 achieved via predictor-based control design techniques and,  l
9 in particular, via exact predictor feedbacks, see, for exam-  s
1 ple, [1,4,7,18]. The baseline, continuous predictor-based de-  a
1 signs are accompanied with certain stability (and robustness)  s
1 guarantees, see, e.g., [4,5,14,18]. However, implementation  b
.
of predictor feedbacks may be subject to digital effects, such  c
4
as, for example, sampling and quantization, which may de-  o
0
4 teriorate closed loop performance of the nominal continuous  j
2 designs, when these effects are left uncompensated, see, for  D
: example, [14,20]. Therefore, addressing issues arising due to
f
v digital implementation of predictor feedbacks is practically
i  m
and theoretically significant, in order to provably preserve  a
X
r the stability guarantees of the original, continuous designs.  a
a  b
Among the potential digital implementation issues arising
c
in implementation of predictor feedbacks, sampled measure-
ments and control inputs applied via zero-order hold have
I
been addressed in [13,24,25]; while [2,29] address sampling
f
in sequential predictors-based designs and [8,10] address
i
sampling in nonlinear systems with state delay. In particu-
s
lar, [13,24,25] introduce design and analysis approaches for

olaos Bekiaris-Liberis
hnical University of Crete, 73100 Chania, Greece
asymptotic stabilization of linear systems with input delay and
form. The control design relies on a quantized version of the
measurements of the plant and actuator states enter the predictor
t the tunable parameter of the quantizer (in a piecewise constant
ase the error of the quantizers. The key element in the proof of
is derivation of solutions’ estimates combining a backstepping
r addressing the error due to quantization. We extend this result
example.
lic PDE cascade, hybrid control.
ompensating the effect of sampling in measurements and
ctuation employing predictor-based designs; while [9] also
ddresses quantization effects in nonlinear systems with de-
ay on the state. Because our design also relies on a switched
trategy (although it is assumed that the input is continuously
pplied and that continuous measurements are available), re-
ults on predictor-based event-triggered control design may
e also viewed as relevant [12,20,21,22,26]. Besides [3] that
onsiders the case of a class of boundary controlled, first-
rder hyperbolic PDEs (Partial Differential Equations) sub-
ect to state quantization, not involving an ODE (Ordinary
ifferential Equation) part, paper [23] presents a predictor-
eedback design for a particular case of a model of robot
anipulator with quantized input, which, however, neither
ddresses a general linear system with a systematic design
nd analysis approach nor aims at achieving asymptotic sta-
ility via a dynamic quantizer, while quantization affects the
ontrol input and not the state measurements.
n the present paper, we develop a switched predictor-
eedback control design that achieves compensation of both
nput delay and state (or input) quantization. The control de-
ign relies on two main ingredients—a baseline predictor-
eedback design and a switching strategy that dynamically
djusts the tunable parameter of the quantizer. In particu-
ar, the nominal predictor state formula is modified such that
he plant and actuator states are replaced by their quantized
ersions; while the tunable parameter of the quantizer is dy-
amically adjusted (in a piecewise manner), in order to ini-
ially increase the range of the quantizer and to subsequently
ecreases its error, as it is done in the case of delay-free sys-
ems in [6,19]. The transition between the two main modes

2.2  Properties of the Quantizer
T
The state X of the plant and the actuator state u are available
only in quantized form. We consider here dynamic quantizers
with an adjustable parameter of the form (see, e.g., [6,19])
(cid:18)  (cid:18) (cid:19)  (cid:18) (cid:19)(cid:19)
X  u

µ  µ

f
where µ > 0 can be manipulated and  this  is  called
n  n  t
“zoom” variable. The quantizers q  : R → R  and q  :
1  2
∞  R  ∞  R
L ([0,D];  ) →L ([0,D];  ) are locally Lipchitz functions
that satisfy the following properties
P1: If ∥(X,u)∥ ≤M, then ∥(q1(X)−X,q2(u)−u)∥ ≤ ∆,
P2: If ∥(X,u)∥ >M, then ∥(q1(X),q2(u))∥ >M−∆,
P3: If ∥(X,u)∥ ≤Mˆ , then q1(X) = 0 and q2(u) = 0,  w
for some positive constants M,Mˆ , and ∆, with M > ∆ and
Mˆ <M. When the argument of the employed quantizer is a
vector, the quantizer function is a vector itself, defined com-
ponentwise according to (14), satisfying properties P1–P3.
In the present case, we consider uniform quantizers for each
element of the vector argument, while we discern between
the quantizer function of the measurements of the plant state
X and the function that corresponds to the actuator state mea-
surements u. For simplicity of control design and analysis
we assume a single tunable parameter µ. In fact, this is also
practically reasonable, considering a case where, e.g., a sin-
gle computer with a single camera collects measurements.  T
The quantizers considered here differ from typical piecewise
δ
constant quantizers, taking finitely many values, in that we
assume they are locally Lipschitz functions. Although this
may appear as a restrictive requirement, in practice, it isn’t
as it is also illustrated in Figure 1, which shows a locally
Lipschitz quantizer that may arbitrarily closely approximate
f
a quantizer with rectilinear quantization regions. This is a
t
technical requirement to guarantee existence and uniqueness
of solutions in a straightforward manner. The stability result
obtained and the stability proof do not essentially rely on
this, suggesting that this assumption could be removed.
3

ain based ISS argument, for time intervals of constant µ.

egion), both dependent on the overshoot M0.

howing that event (18) is detected with the initial condi-
ions (X0,u0). We present in Figure 2 the norm of the state
X,u) of the closed-loop system along with the switching
ignal µ(t)MM, as well as the response of the ODE/actuator
tates. Specifically, for x = D, we display the control input
ignal. The response u of the closed-loop system is com-
uted numerically using a Lax-Friedrichs scheme with time
nd spatial discretization steps set to 0.01 and 0.02, respec-
ively. The integral involved in the backstepping controller
15) is numerically computed using the trapezoidal rule.
o further elucidate the importance of the proposed con-
rol design methodology, we present in Figures 3 and 4 the
esponses of the closed-loop systems when employing the
ominal controller, without compensating for the effects of
tate measurements quantization. For a fixed, small value of
(µ = 0.1), we note that the states of the closed-loop sys-
em grow unbounded under the nominal control law, as de-
icted in Figure 3. This occurs because the initial condition
ies outside the range of the quantizer, which is defined as
µ, and thus, the quantizer and control input saturate. Con-
ersely, for a fixed, large value of µ (µ = 100), the states
f the closed-loop system remain bounded under the nom-
nal control law, owing to the input-to-state stability prop-
rty of the nominal backstepping controller with respect to
boundary disturbance, as the quantizer does not saturate.
owever, achieving asymptotic stabilization is unattainable,
s illustrated in Figure 4, due to significant quantization er-
or ∆µ. In fact,  the system appears to enter a limit cycle
ecause, due to the quantization effect, the control input be-
omes negligible when the state lies within a certain region
round zero, leading to state growth (since the open-loop sys-
em is unstable), until the quantizer switches to a non-zero
alue. This behavior aligns with findings reported for finite-
imensional systems (see, e.g., [19,28]), hyperbolic systems
as discussed in [3,11,27]) and parabolic systems (see [17]),
hich do not explicitly aim to compensate for quantization
ffects to achieve asymptotic stabilization.

lobal asymptotic stability of linear systems with input de-
ay and subject  to both state and input quantization has
een established,  thanks to a switched predictor-feedback
ontrol  law that we  introduced. The proof  strategy uti-
ized the backstepping method along with small-gain and
nput-to-state stability arguments. Future works should incor-
orate state-dependent event-based switching mechanisms
ince the switching parameter in this paper operates as a
ime-dependent periodic mechanism. Moreover, considera-
ion should be given to nonlinear time-delay systems, and
ther classes of PDEs, such as parabolic equations, and cas-
ade parabolic-ODE systems, subject to quantization.

Transactions on Automatic control, 27(4):869–879, 1982.
